narrative:
**HEADLINE**
Overall macro risk rose to 7.1 as sea-lane vulnerabilities escalated, offsetting declines in Japan carry-trade and emerging-market debt pressures.

**CHANGED STORIES**

• **Sea lanes & undersea cables** (6→7, High): Escalated on unspecified geopolitical or infrastructure trigger; elevates supply-chain disruption risk for critical trade and digital connectivity.

• **Japan fiscal/JGB/carry trade** (7→6, Elevated): 30-year JGB yield rose 50bp to 3.86%, reducing yen funding cost advantage and easing near-term unwind pressure on leveraged positions.

• **Developing-world debt crisis** (6→5, Elevated): US real 10y yield at 2.35% and zero new IMF programs/missed coupons signal reduced refinancing stress, though backdrop remains fragile.

• **Food & fertilizer fragility** (7→6, Elevated): China's tightened fertilizer export curbs persist; risk downgrade reflects data lag rather than policy reversal.

**TOP WATCH**
Private credit's opaque expansion (8, High) remains the highest-risk outlier; no driver data suggests structural opacity in leverage is unmonitored.

stories.name: Treasury basis trade (hidden leverage)

indicators.label: SOFR minus EFFR (%)
MOVE index (Treasury volatility)
SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)

stories.summary: Hedge funds run a highly leveraged arbitrage between Treasury cash bonds and futures. It is a major source of liquidity, but a spike in funding costs or Treasury volatility can force rapid unwinds, as briefly happened in March 2020 and again in 2025.

stories.name: Japan fiscal / JGB / carry trade

indicators.label: USD/JPY (yen strengthening = lower = risk)
30-year JGB yield (%)
Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)

stories.summary: Decades of near-zero rates made Japan the funding leg of a vast global carry trade. As the BoJ normalizes and JGB yields rise, fiscal financing gets harder and carry unwinds can transmit stress worldwide - the August 2024 mini-crash was a preview.

stories.name: Private credit's opaque expansion

indicators.label: US high-yield OAS (%, ≈310bps)
Direct-lending default rate (%, Fitch incl. distressed exchanges)
Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)

stories.summary: Lending has migrated from banks to lightly regulated private-credit funds, where assets are marked infrequently and leverage is opaque. Defaults are rising from a low base; the risk is that losses surface suddenly rather than being visible in real time.

stories.name: Developing-world debt crisis

indicators.label: Broad US dollar index (FRED DTWEXBGS)
US 10y real yield (TIPS, %)
New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A strong dollar, high real rates, and heavy foreign-currency debt loads leave many developing economies vulnerable to a sudden stop. A cluster of sovereign defaults or new IMF programs would signal the slow-burn crisis turning acute.

stories.name: China's price spillover (deflation or cost-push)

indicators.label: China PPI YoY (%) - large swing either way is worse
USD/CNY (yuan depreciation = higher = risk)
New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)

stories.summary: China's factory-gate prices spill into global goods markets in BOTH directions: years of PPI deflation exported disinflation and squeezed competitors, while a sharp 2026 reversal to positive PPI (energy/commodity cost-push) now exports input-cost pressure. Either large swing is destabilising; the indicator is scored on the size of the move, not its sign.

stories.name: Critical minerals as a weapon

indicators.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)

stories.summary: China dominates the mining and especially the processing of rare earths and critical minerals, and has shown willingness to use export licensing as leverage. Supply chains for magnets and defense inputs have little near-term substitute.

stories.name: Stablecoins & Treasury demand

indicators.label: Largest stablecoin max peg deviation (%)
Total USD stablecoin supply ($bn)
GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Dollar stablecoins have become sizeable holders of short-term Treasuries, linking crypto plumbing to the funding market. A large redemption wave could force rapid T-bill selling; the GENIUS Act's implementation is the key regulatory variable.

stories.name: AI build-out circular financing

indicators.label: US investment-grade OAS (%) - big-issuer proxy
AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)

stories.summary: AI infrastructure spending vastly exceeds current AI revenue, increasingly financed with debt and circular vendor arrangements. If monetization disappoints or GPU-backed financing strains, the capex cycle could reverse sharply.

stories.name: CRE maturity wall / regional banks

indicators.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A wall of commercial real-estate loans - especially office - matures into higher rates and lower valuations, concentrated at regional banks. Rising delinquencies and 'extend and pretend' practices defer, rather than resolve, the losses.

stories.name: France / eurozone fragility

indicators.label: OAT-Bund 10y spread (%)
Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)

stories.summary: France combines a wide deficit, a fragmented parliament, and recurring budget standoffs, widening its spread over Germany. Political instability or a rating downgrade would test eurozone cohesion at its second-largest economy.

stories.name: Hidden consumer debt

indicators.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
US unemployment rate (%)
BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)

stories.summary: Beneath headline figures, subprime auto and buy-now-pay-later delinquencies are climbing, and the labor market is the swing factor. A rise in unemployment would convert stretched-but-current borrowers into defaults quickly.

stories.name: Gold / de-dollarization

indicators.label: Gold price (USD/oz)
USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)

stories.summary: Central banks are accumulating gold and building non-dollar settlement arrangements at the margin. It is a slow erosion rather than a sudden shift, but reserve 'weaponization' events can accelerate diversification away from the dollar.

stories.name: Insurance retreat

indicators.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
US single-family mortgage delinquency (%, FRED DRSFRMACBS)
Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)

stories.summary: Insurers are retreating from climate-exposed markets through non-renewals and exits, shifting risk onto homeowners and state backstops. Severe catastrophe seasons strain the system and feed into housing and mortgage stress.

stories.name: Pensions / demographics

indicators.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
Large public-pension funded ratio (%)

stories.summary: Aging demographics and underfunded public pensions create slow fiscal pressure, while leveraged liability-driven strategies can blow up fast - as the UK gilt/LDI crisis showed in 2022. Benefit-reform fights are the political pressure valve.

stories.name: AI power & the grid bottleneck

indicators.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)

stories.summary: Data-center electricity demand is colliding with multi-year grid interconnection queues and slow capacity additions. Whether this is an acute shortfall or eases depends on the pace of AI build-out versus new supply - forecasts diverge widely.

stories.name: Copper & the electrification deficit

indicators.label: Copper price (USD/lb, COMEX HG=F)
Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)

stories.summary: Electrification and AI infrastructure point to structural copper deficits as demand outruns new mine supply, which takes a decade to build. Price is the live signal; mine disruptions and inventory draws would confirm tightening.

stories.name: Water - the underpriced input

indicators.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)

stories.summary: Water is systematically underpriced yet essential to chip fabrication, agriculture, and power. Droughts in key hubs (Taiwan's fabs, major farming regions) and transboundary disputes can disrupt supply chains in ways markets rarely price in advance.

stories.name: Food & fertilizer fragility

indicators.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)

stories.summary: Food security depends on concentrated fertilizer supply chains - potash, phosphate, and gas-derived nitrogen - exposed to export curbs and shipping chokepoints. Curbs or input shocks feed through to prices with a lag.

stories.name: The lithography (ASML) chokepoint

indicators.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)

stories.summary: ASML is the sole maker of EUV lithography machines, with Zeiss the sole optics supplier - a single point of failure for advanced chips. Export controls, retaliation, or any disruption to this chain would ripple across the entire tech economy.

stories.name: Sea lanes & undersea cables

indicators.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
WTI crude oil (USD/bbl, CL=F)
Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)

stories.summary: A few maritime chokepoints and undersea cables carry most global trade and data. The 2026 Hormuz/Red Sea disruptions showed how quickly closures spike energy and shipping costs; cable cuts are a parallel, under-watched vulnerability.

stories.name: Clearinghouses (financial chokepoint)

indicators.label: Equity volatility (VIX)
Treasury volatility (MOVE)
Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Central counterparties now concentrate the system's risk by design, and Treasury central-clearing mandates will route more activity through them. In a volatility spike, procyclical margin calls can amplify stress - they are a systemic single point of failure.

items.label: Direct-lending default rate (%, Fitch incl. distressed exchanges)
items.rationale: The latest confirmed Fitch PCDR print (inclusive of distressed exchanges and PIK/maturity-extension events) is 6.0% TTM through May 2026, unchanged from the April 2026 record; a LinkedIn headline suggests a possible subsequent drop to 5.2% for June data but is unverifiable from primary sources and conflicts with LSTA's June 15 report being the most recent Fitch PCDR release listed, so the rubric's anchor is held at 6.0 pending a confirmed new print.
items.fact: Fitch Ratings' U.S. Private Credit Default Rate Remains at Record High 6.0% in May 2026, published June 15 2026 — per LSTA relay of Fitch commentary (https://www.lsta.org/content/fitch-ratings-commentary-page/) and corroborated by ZeroHedge/Epoch Times citing Fitch directly; Fitch monitored ~1,500 issuers and logged 14 default events in May, with maturity extensions under stress as the dominant default type.

dissent.view: LinkedIn post title from Fitch Ratings account states 'U.S. Private Credit Default Rate drops to 5.2% in July' — content unscrapable; timing ambiguous (could be June-data report or reference July 2025 5.2% print per Connect Money Aug 2025 article); not corroborated by LSTA or any primary source as of 2026-07-20

Proskauer Private Credit Default Index: 2.73% in Q1 2026 (count-based, excludes many distressed exchanges) — materially lower because it uses a narrower, loan-count methodology rather than Fitch's inclusive issuer-volume measure

Moody's: distressed restructurings accounted for ~65% of all 2025 private credit defaults; excluding them yields headline rates of 1.6-4.7% — Moody's does not publish a single composite PCDR figure comparable to Fitch's

items.label: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
items.rationale: The rubric scores 2 = PROBLEMS when the deadline is missed without final rules. The July 18, 2026 statutory deadline has passed with zero final rules issued by any of the six mandated agencies, constituting a clear deadline miss; no court stay is present but the deadline-miss criterion is independently sufficient for a score of 2.
items.fact: As of July 18, 2026 (the statutory one-year deadline), not one of the federal agencies mandated by the GENIUS Act (OCC, FDIC, Federal Reserve, NCUA, Treasury/FinCEN/OFAC) issued final implementing rules; all agencies remained at the proposed/NPRM stage, with several comment periods still open past the deadline (e.g. FDIC BSA/sanctions proposal open through Aug 4, joint CIP proposal open through Aug 21). Source: gncrypto.news 'U.S. Regulators Miss GENIUS Act Deadline' (2026-07-18); cryptobriefing.com (2026-07-19); blockonomi.com (2026-07-18); fdic.gov NPRM (Apr 2026); occ.gov bulletin (Jun 2026).

items.label: Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: Seven or more named funds — with combined AUM well above $100bn — simultaneously enforced quarterly repurchase caps in the same two-quarter window (Q1-Q2 2026), clearly satisfying the rubric's threshold of >=3 largest funds or combined gated AUM >$100bn, mapping mechanically to score 2 (WIDESPREAD). No corroborating funding-market dislocation (SOFR-EFFR, MOVE, repo) has reached systemic-crisis levels, but the breadth of gating across every major manager is itself the corroborating market signal that structural liquidity stress is real and widespread.
items.fact: In Q1 and Q2 2026, at least 7 named large private-credit/BDC funds enforced quarterly redemption caps at the contractual 5% ceiling after requests far exceeded that limit: Blackstone BCRED (~$79-82B, 10% Q2 requests gated at 5%, first formal gate in Q2; 7.9% in Q1 fulfilled above cap); Apollo Debt Solutions (~$25B, 16.8% Q2 requests capped at 5%); Ares Strategic Income Fund (~$21.5B, 14.6% Q2 / 11.6% Q1 requests capped at 5%); BlackRock/HPS Corporate Lending (~$26B, 9.3% Q1 requests capped at 5%); Blue Owl OCIC/OTIC (22%/41% Q1 requests capped at 5%; OBDC II permanently closed); Cliffwater Corporate Lending Fund (~$31B, 17% Q2 requests with ~1/3 fulfilled); North Haven Private Income Fund (11.6% Q2 requests, capped at 5%). Congressional Research Service (CRS IN12674, updated April 2 2026), CNBC 2026-06-23, Stanger May 14 2026, Sekond/PitchBook/ZeroHedge June-July 2026 corroborate across primary filings.

dissent.view: Stanger (May 14 2026) states 'no NAV BDC has gated redemptions' in the strict sense — funds exceeded their 5% caps voluntarily or by fulfilling 100%+ of requests; the gates are contractual caps working as designed, not emergency suspensions. R.A. Stanger press release May 14 2026.

Federal Reserve May 2026 Financial Stability Report characterized Q1 redemption activity as 'limited and manageable', suggesting regulators do not view this as a systemic crisis. Lead Lag Report / LeadLag citing Fed FSR May 2026.

items.label: SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)
items.rationale: Rubric score 1 requires 'an official extension granted OR exemptive-relief requests pending.' Both conditions are satisfied: the Feb 2025 extension was the first (and only) formal deadline shift, and as of Apr-May 2026 SIFMA and IIB exemptive-relief requests are actively pending before the SEC. No second formal extension has been announced, so score 2 is not triggered.
items.fact: The SEC granted ONE formal one-year extension on Feb 25, 2025 (FR effective Mar 4, 2025), shifting cash to Dec 31, 2026 and repo to Jun 30, 2027. Additionally, on Apr 17, 2026, the SEC published notices of pending exemptive-relief requests from SIFMA (inter-affiliate scope expansion) and IIB (cross-border transactions), with a public comment deadline of May 29, 2026 — sources: sec.gov/featured-topics/treasury-clearing-implementation (updated ~Jul 2026); sec.gov/newsroom/speeches-statements/uyeda-update-sec-work-toward-treasury-clearing-implementation-042026 (Apr 20, 2026); home.treasury.gov/system/files/221/TBACCharge1Q22026.pdf (May 2026). No second deadline extension has been announced.

dissent.view: DTCC and some industry sources frame the mandate as broadly on track, with FICC infrastructure 'ready' and volumes growing, suggesting no further slippage beyond the already-granted extension; Risk.net (Mar 2026) states 'the path to compliance is clear'

items.label: Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)
items.rationale: The cumulative 10y JGB yield rise linked to Takaichi fiscal concerns exceeds 25bps (>70bps YTD, ~60bps since March) and the funding path is credibility-contested (UNCLEAR rubric trigger), but auctions are clearing well above bid-to-cover thresholds with no failed sale, no emergency BOJ op, and no single-event >50bp spike, ruling out DISORDERLY (score 2).
items.fact: 10y JGB yield was ~2.781% as of July 14 2026 (peak 2.901%), up over 70bps YTD partly on Takaichi fiscal concern; most recent JGB auctions clearing strongly (30y July 7 bid-to-cover 4.55x, 20y ~July 14 bid-to-cover 4.52x, tail=0); Takaichi's May 25 ~3 trillion yen supplementary budget claims no extra net issuance but funding path credibility is widely disputed. Sources: CNBC July 14 2026; Business Recorder ~July 14 2026; Dukascopy July 7 2026.

dissent.view: Oxford Economics (June 12) frames the yield rise (2.1% to 2.7%) as a mix of global repricing and fiscal concern, with a 'recent slight retreat' after a smaller-than-expected supplementary budget — suggesting the fiscal shock is partially priced and partially stabilising

Lazard (mid-year 2026) notes Takaichi 'tempered expectations for sizable fiscal stimulus, in part to reassure jittery bond markets' — suggesting the fiscal impulse is being managed downward, consistent with a borderline 0/1 reading

items.label: Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)
items.rationale: The rubric's FRICTION criteria are met on multiple counts: a documented documentation backlog in bilateral legal agreements (TBAC May 2026), pending exemptive relief on inter-affiliate and extraterritorial scope (SEC/SIFMA, Apr-Jun 2026), and industry-survey data showing fewer than one-third of firms 'very familiar' with done-away clearing — well below the 60% confidence threshold in the rubric. No deadline extension or confirmed operational failure has occurred, so level 2 is not warranted.
items.fact: TBAC May 2026 presentation (home.treasury.gov/system/files/221/TBACCharge1Q22026.pdf, dated May 2026) states: 'Largest challenge from an implementation standpoint continues to be the amount of legal work and documentation associated with the bilateral agreements between participants'; the SIFMA/ValueExchange pulse survey cited by TBAC found that 'operational readiness and re-papering contracts were cited as the biggest worries for up to 71% of firms' and 'fewer than one in three firms report being very familiar with done-away clearing'; as of April-June 2026, two major exemptive-relief requests (SIFMA inter-affiliate, IIB extraterritorial) remain pending and unresolved before the SEC, with ICMA and SIFMA both flagging outstanding regulatory clarity as a prerequisite for operational builds. SEC Chair Atkins confirmed the Dec 31 2026 cash deadline stands (Bloomberg, Mar 10 2026) and the SEC stated it does not intend further extensions (SEC.gov, Sep 30 2025).

dissent.view: SEC Chair Atkins (Mar 10 2026) reaffirmed the Dec 2026 deadline and TBAC notes cash clearing is 'relatively better positioned' than repo, suggesting cash-side friction may be manageable; some sell-side majors (JPM, BofA, State Street) are operationally advanced.

items.label: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
items.rationale: Across the hyperscaler group capex growth (49-107% YoY) outpaces AI-attributable revenue growth (28-63% YoY) at Alphabet, Amazon, and Meta; however AI revenues are still strongly accelerating YoY at all four companies with no QoQ deceleration reported, placing this squarely in rubric level 1 (WIDE). Microsoft is the partial outlier with AI revenue growing 123% YoY vs 49% capex growth, but the group aggregate remains capex-growth-dominant.
items.fact: Alphabet Q1 2026: capex $35.7B (+107% YoY), Google Cloud revenue $20.0B (+63% YoY) — SEC 8-K, April 29 2026. Microsoft FQ3 2026: capex $31.9B (+49% YoY), AI annualized revenue run rate $37B (+123% YoY) — CNBC/Microsoft IR, April 30 2026. Amazon Q1 2026: capex $44.2B, AWS +28% YoY. Combined 2026 hyperscaler capex guided ~$700-750B, up ~67-77% YoY vs AI cloud revenue growth of 28-63% YoY.

dissent.view: Microsoft AI revenue (+123% YoY) outpaces its own capex growth (+49% FQ3 YoY), suggesting CLOSING (score 0) on a company-specific basis
CreditSights flags capex reaching 46-54% of revenue for Meta and Alphabet as 'seemingly untenable', implying risk of WIDENING FAST if revenue decelerates

items.label: BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: Affirm 30+ DPD rose +40bps YoY (2.4%→2.8%), satisfying the RISING criterion of delinquency up YoY; charge-offs remain ~1.83% per CFPB, well below the 3% threshold, so the rubric maps to 1 not 2. The +40bps is also below the 100bps spike threshold required for a score of 2.
items.fact: Affirm SEC 8-K FQ3'26 Shareholder Letter (period ending March 31, 2026): 30+ DPD on Monthly Installment Loans ex-Peloton = 2.8%, vs 2.4% in the same quarter FY2025, a +40bps YoY rise. CFPB/Richmond Fed (Feb 2026) put the latest BNPL industry charge-off rate at 1.83% (2023), well below 3%. Source: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0001820953/000162828026032105/affirmfq326shareholderle.htm and https://www.richmondfed.org/publications/research/economic_brief/2026/eb_26-05

dissent.view: Klarna global BNPL delinquency rate fell to 0.88% in Q2 2025 (-15bps YoY), suggesting STABLE (score=0) for that provider; Klarna's short-term BNPL delinquency remains sub-1% and improving.

LendingTree March 2026 consumer survey reports 47% of BNPL users paid late in the past year (up from 41% in 2025), which if treated as a delinquency proxy could imply more stress; however this is self-reported behavior, not a lender-reported 30+ DPD series.

items.label: GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)
items.rationale: A named borrower (CoreWeave) faced an identified 2026 refinancing wall on GPU-backed debt against sharply depreciated collateral -- a textbook rubric-1 stress signal. The wall was resolved via new facilities, not a default or distressed exchange, so rubric-2 is not warranted; the idiosyncratic refinancing pressure and flagged structural risks (QTS bullet-structure spread widening per Citi/Forbes June 2026, GPU depreciation warnings across sector) keep the score at 1.
items.fact: CoreWeave carried a '$4.2 billion refinancing wall due in 2026' on GPU-collateralized debt with collateral values (H100 GPU rental rates) down ~70-90% since 2023 (Quinn Emanuel client alert, March 2026; Wedbush/Finterra, Feb 23 2026). CoreWeave successfully navigated the wall: it closed the $8.5B DDTL 4.0 (rated A3/Moody's -- first IG GPU-backed facility) on March 31 2026 (SEC filing / CoreWeave IR), and the $3.1B DDTL 5.0 (Ba2/Moody's, oversubscribed, priced 50bps tighter) on May 18 2026 (CoreWeave IR). No default, distressed exchange, or failed refinancing was confirmed at any named issuer as of the research date.

dissent.view: Some analysts frame CoreWeave's successive new facilities as evidence that GPU-backed debt is now normalizing and attracting IG institutional capital at scale, arguing the market has moved beyond stress to routine execution -- consistent with a score of 0.

items.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
items.rationale: Thirteen states with documented non-renewal waves places the indicator firmly in the RISING band (5-15 states); however, AM Best stability upgrade, softening reinsurance, and new carrier entrants in FL preclude classification as ACCELERATING, and the rubric threshold for level 2 (>15 states or full top-10 exit) is not met.
items.fact: A June 2026 analysis (climatecosmos.com, citing NAIC MCAS data via Weiss Ratings) identifies 13 US states with active insurer non-renewal waves in 2026, including FL (3.35% non-renewal rate, highest in nation), CA (3.18%, 3.9x its 2018 rate), TX, CO, NE, OK, NC, MN, GA, KS, LA, and others. NAIC issued a nationwide data call in March 2026 covering cancellations/non-renewals across all 50 states. AM Best revised the US homeowners segment outlook from negative to stable (December 2025), and reinsurance rates fell ~10-25% at June 1, 2026 renewals (Openly mid-year 2026 outlook). No top-10 insurer has fully exited a major state market in 2026; State Farm remains present in CA (~72K non-renewals but not a full exit); Florida added ~20 new approved carriers (Insurance Journal, May 2026).

dissent.view: insurance.com survey (May 2026) reports only 10% of homeowners nationally had policies canceled or non-renewed, with carrier exits cited by only 3% of respondents, suggesting the consumer-level impact may be more limited than state-count metrics imply

AM Best (Dec 2025) and HousingWire/Openly (mid-2026) characterize the national homeowners market as stabilizing, which could argue for a STABLE rating if state-count non-renewals are viewed as contained and trending toward resolution

items.label: Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: At least one public FDIC enforcement/consent action against a subsequently failed community bank (<$10bn) is confirmed within the trailing 6 months, satisfying the ISOLATED rubric threshold of >=1 enforcement action citing bank stress; zero confirmed CRE-concentration language in primary failure documents prevents escalation to CLUSTER, and no failure exceeded $10bn assets or triggered coordinated interagency action. [cross-check (asi1): two reads disagreed (1 vs 0); held for review]
items.fact: FDIC failed-bank list (last updated July 10, 2026) records 3 US bank failures YTD 2026: Metropolitan Capital Bank & Trust ($261M, Jan 30 - FDIC OIG cites C&I asset-quality issues, not CRE); Community Bank & Trust - West Georgia ($288M, May 1 - KBRA cites a Jan 12 2026 FDIC consent order and loan-quality issues; no primary FDIC/OCC document explicitly names CRE concentration as cause); Kentland Federal Savings ($3.7M, Jul 10 - OCC cites unsafe practices/capital depletion, no CRE link). No single failure exceeded $10bn assets. The Jan 12 2026 FDIC consent order against Community Bank & Trust - West Georgia is a confirmed public enforcement action preceding that bank's failure; whether it explicitly cites CRE concentration is unconfirmed in truncated KBRA excerpt. Sources: fdic.gov/resources/resolutions/bank-failures/in-brief/2026 (Jul 10 2026); fdicoig.gov (May 29 2026); kbra.com (May 2026); americanbanker.com (Jul 10 2026).

items.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
items.rationale: The Fitch subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency index hit 6.90% in January 2026 — a 32-year record — directly satisfying the rubric; the February 2026 reading of 6.80% confirms sustained elevation with only modest seasonal normalization, and no more recent Fitch monthly figure has been publicly reported. [vintage guard: new read is 2026-01 data but the prior applied value was 2026-02 data; kept the newer vintage]
items.fact: Fitch Ratings subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency index: 6.90% in January 2026 (record high, +34 bps YoY), edging down to 6.80% in February 2026; per Wolf Street (May 19, 2026) citing Fitch Ratings data, and corroborated by Symend (June 2026 update) and Auto Finance News (Jan 2026).

dissent.view: 6.80% for February 2026 (Fitch Ratings, per Wolf Street May 19 2026) — the next confirmed monthly data point after the January peak
6.74% for December 2025 (Fitch Ratings, per Auto Finance News Jan 16 2026) — the pre-peak December figure
~6% from mid-2024 to late 2025 (Philadelphia Fed Apr 2026 report citing NY Fed CCP/Equifax data) — consumer-level data vs. ABS-pool data yields lower absolute rate

items.label: Large public-pension funded ratio (%)
items.rationale: Milliman's PPFI is the preferred primary source per rubric instructions and the April 30, 2026 reading of 87.6% is the most recent published figure for the 100 largest US public defined benefit plans; it maps directly to the rubric's requested representative funded ratio as a percent.
items.fact: Milliman 100 Public Pension Funding Index (PPFI): funded ratio of the 100 largest U.S. public pension plans stood at 87.6% as of April 30, 2026 — per Milliman PPFI May 2026 release dated June 3, 2026 (https://www.milliman.com/en/insight/public-pension-funding-index-may-2026).

dissent.view: Equable Institute estimates the broader national aggregate funded ratio for all US state and local retirement systems improved to 82.5% for fiscal year 2025 (based on data through December 31, 2025) — a wider universe than Milliman's top-100 and measured at an earlier date.

items.label: New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: Rubric requires >=1 new IMF program OR a missed coupon for RISING (1); none found in the window — all IMF actions are reviews of programs approved before Jan 2026. No missed-coupon event identified. Count = 0 → CALM.
items.fact: In the trailing 6 months (Jan 20 – Jul 20, 2026), IMF.org news shows only review completions for pre-existing arrangements (Argentina EFF 2nd review May 2026, Ecuador EFF 5th review Apr 2026, Ethiopia ECF 5th review Jul 2026, Ukraine EFF 1st review staff-level agreement Jun 2026). No new IMF lending arrangements approved from scratch and no sovereign missed coupon or standstill event were found in this window. Source: imf.org/en/news, multiple press releases Apr–Jul 2026.

dissent.view: Republic of Congo classified as 'debt in distress' with newly accumulated arrears per IMF Mar 2026 Post-Financing Assessment; Senegal's IMF program remained suspended into early 2026 with external payment pressures — these are stress indicators that could shade toward RISING if reclassified as new distress events.

items.label: Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)
items.rationale: No new sovereign reserve freeze or outright seizure has occurred in 2026; the Russia freeze is a continuation, not a new action. BRICS Pay and The Unit represent active official rhetoric and pilot-stage launches—matching Band 1 (TALK/new non-dollar settlement agreements and launches) but not Band 2, which requires an executed new freeze or a major economy operationally off USD in reserves.
items.fact: As of July 2026, no new sovereign reserve freeze or seizure has been executed beyond the pre-existing Russia CBR freeze (~$280bn, since 2022). On non-dollar settlement, BRICS Pay is in early operational/pilot phase: Russian FM Lavrov (Rio summit, 2025) pegged the pilot launch to end-2026; Russian Deputy FM Ryabkov pegs full operational status to 2030; BRICS Pay tracking site notes 'as of mid-2025 the system remains in the planning and early-pilot stage.' The Unit gold-backed token ran a 100-unit pilot (Oct 2025) but no confirmed full launch. EBC Financial Group (April 13, 2026): 'As of 13 April 2026, there is no official BRICS common currency in circulation and no announced launch date for one.' Sources: bricspayment.ru/en/news; ebc.com; brookings.edu (Jun 2025); reuters.com/cfr.org context.

dissent.view: Some pro-BRICS sources (watcher.guru, informedclearly.com, Jan-Jun 2026) claim BRICS Pay is already in 'decisive operational phase' in 2026 with 60-67% intra-bloc local-currency trade—implying Band 2 ACTION on settlement shift; however these sources are not authoritative primary sources and conflict with official BRICS/BIS/central bank statements.

EU's invocation of Article 122 (Dec 2025) to make Russia asset freeze indefinite has been characterized by some analysts as crossing into quasi-seizure territory; however all authoritative sources (CFR, Brookings, EPRS, ECB) confirm principal ownership remains Russian and outright confiscation has not been executed.

items.label: USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
items.rationale: The rubric requires the latest published USD share of allocated global FX reserves as a percent; the IMF COFER Data Brief of July 1, 2026 reports 57.13% for 2026Q1, which is the most recent official release. Note that from 2025Q3 onward the IMF eliminated the 'unallocated' residual, so this figure now covers 100% of world reserves rather than only the historically-defined allocated subset.
items.fact: Per IMF COFER Data Brief published July 1, 2026: 'The share of US dollar holdings in foreign exchange reserves increased to 57.13 percent in 2026Q1, from 56.42 percent in 2025Q4.' (source: https://data.imf.org/en/news/imf%20data%20brief%20july%201)

dissent.view: coferdata.com displays USD at 56.77% (reflecting 2025Q4, likely not yet updated to the July 1 2026 release)

items.label: Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)
items.rationale: The load-bearing Argus/Reuters figure of x4-to-x5 for ex-China heavy rare earth oxide prices vs Chinese domestic, corroborated by Benchmark's 4.4x CIF North America reading and analyst consensus of 4-6x, maps directly to the rubric's request for the premium multiple; midpoint 4.5 is the best single reading. FOB China spreads (~1.3-1.5x) are narrower but do not reflect landed Western cost, so the CIF/North America multiple is the appropriate anchor.
items.fact: Argus data cited by Reuters (Oregon Group, June 10 2026): dysprosium and terbium oxide prices outside China 'climbed x4 to x5' since China's April 2025 export controls took effect, with dysprosium ex-China ~$1,450/kg vs ~$209-262/kg domestic China. Benchmark Minerals (March 2026) pegged CIF North America dysprosium at 4.4x EXW China in 2025, rising. Multiple industry sources for mid-2026 converge on 4-5x for CIF/landed Western price vs domestic China benchmark.

dissent.view: FOB terbium $1,483/kg vs domestic $1,179.54/kg (July 2026 SMM) implies only ~1.26x FOB/domestic spread — a much lower multiple if FOB China rather than CIF Western is used as the ex-China price

Project Blue/Discovery Alert (May 2026): ex-China monazite concentrate premium only ~2.6x Chinese benchmark — but this is a concentrate-level blended figure diluted by low-value light REEs, not a pure HREE oxide multiple
Benchmark Minerals (March 2026) forecasts the multiple rising to 8.3x by 2027, suggesting the current 4.4-4.5x reading may be conservative if export licence tightening accelerates
Physical/retail terbium ex-China at $4,028.50/kg (Jul 10 2026) vs ~$1,179/kg domestic implies ~3.4x — retail market, not industrial contract price

items.label: Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
items.rationale: Both the US/allied side (MATCH Act proposal, BIS guidance tightening enforcement) and the Chinese side (April supply-chain retaliation regulations, June tech-transfer retaliation law, June MOFCOM entity-list additions) enacted distinct countermeasures within the trailing 3 months, satisfying the rubric's threshold for level 2 (RETALIATION). Market signal — ASML China revenue nearly halved in one quarter — corroborates the escalation reading.
items.fact: Within the trailing 3 months (Apr–Jul 2026): US side — (1) April 3 2026: bipartisan MATCH Act introduced in Congress to extend DUV lithography and allied-equipment controls targeting ASML/Nikon/Canon sales to China (CNBC/CSIS, April 2026); (2) June 1 2026: BIS issued guidance affirming advanced AI-chip licensing requirements apply to Chinese-HQ'd firms outside China (Al Jazeera, June 1 2026). China side — (3) April 2026: China issued 'Regulations on Industrial Chain and Supply Chain Security,' creating a unified legal retaliation framework (CSIS, April 17 2026); (4) June 11 2026: China enacted expansive tech-transfer controls granting explicit statutory retaliation authority against nations restricting Chinese tech investment (abhs.in citing FT, June 12 2026); (5) June 22 2026: China MOFCOM placed 10 US firms on its export-control blacklist and barred 46 US defence contractors from government procurement in explicit retaliation for Pentagon 1260H list expansion (CNBC/Al Jazeera/Yahoo Finance, June 22 2026). Market corroboration: ASML China revenue share fell from 36% in Q4 2025 to 19% in Q1 2026, confirming material volume impact (ASML/CryptoBriefing, July 2026).

dissent.view: East Asia Forum (March 2026) argued the Trump White House is cooling active US escalation, suspending the Affiliates Rule and relaxing H200 licensing, suggesting effective posture is closer to level 1 (tightening enforcement, not new rules).

CNBC and CSIS analysts characterise China's June 22 entity-list additions as 'largely symbolic' retaliation targeted at defence firms with little China exposure, which could argue against treating them as substantive counter-controls.

items.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
items.rationale: Transit volume is at ~11% of pre-crisis normal (well under 25%), the ceasefire has collapsed with active IRGC attacks on vessels and no functioning convoy workaround per AIS data, and Brent crude up ~30% is fully consistent with a near-closure market signal — all three rubric conditions for a 2 are met. The only mitigating factor is active diplomatic mediation reported July 20 and a US claim that some vessels are still transiting dark, but the volume collapse and corroborating oil price signal dominate.
items.fact: IMF PortWatch recorded 10 vessel transits on July 12 vs. ~88/day baseline (~11% of normal); Kpler counted 14 crossings on July 9; the June 17-18 US-Iran MOU ceasefire has effectively collapsed per Iran (July 19), the US reinstated its naval blockade on July 14, and the IRGC declared all maritime traffic banned and stopped 4 vessels on July 18; Brent crude trading ~$80-90/bbl, up ~30% from July lows (TradingEconomics, 2026-07-20; IMF PortWatch via straits.live, 2026-07-12; EIA STEO, 2026-07-07; UN News, 2026-07-10).

items.label: Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
items.rationale: The rubric classifies '1' for a partial breach; China's April-2025 controls — never lifted — represent exactly that, corroborated by persistent ~50% volume suppression in heavy REE exports. Nominal expiry is ~113 days away (above 90-day threshold) but the unresolved partial breach and absence of a confirmed renewal tip the indicator to EXPIRING/fraying rather than EXTENDED/holding.
items.fact: The Nov-10-2025 MOFCOM suspension of China's Oct-9-2025 rare-earth export controls remains nominally in force through Nov-10-2026 (White House Fact Sheet, Nov-5-2025; confirmed 'still in effect' by senior US official, Reuters/E&E News, May-13-2026). However, China's April-2025 Announcement No.18 controls on heavy REEs (dysprosium, terbium, yttrium, scandium) were never suspended under the truce and remain fully active; Chinese customs data as of May-2026 shows exports of yttrium, dysprosium and terbium still ~50% below pre-April-2025 levels (Reuters/Mining Weekly, May-13-2026; CSIS data via TechTimes, May-27-2026). No formal extension of the Oct-2025 suspension has been announced as of the July-2026 Trump-Xi summit period.

dissent.view: Truce is holding: White House and senior US officials characterise the Oct-2025 suspension as 'still in effect' and frame the April-2025 controls as a separate pre-truce measure outside the agreement's scope, implying no breach of the actual truce terms.
ITI (May-7-2026) notes China 'appears to be upholding its commitments' on the Oct-2025 suspension, but flags license delays/denials as a fraying signal rather than a formal breach.

items.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
items.rationale: The regime is strictly case-by-case (general licences cover only three named producers, not broad volume); statutory review is 45 working days but real approvals run 60–120 days, far exceeding the rubric's 30-day threshold; and new categories (samarium/gadolinium/lutetium compounds) were added to the control list in January 2026—all of which satisfy the rubric's SLOWING criteria. A score of 2 (FROZEN) is not warranted because licences continue to be issued for legitimate civilian commercial flows and no blanket categorical denial to an entire country or sector has been announced (the US-entity blacklisting targets named firms, not a country-wide freeze).
items.fact: China's April 2025 MOFCOM Announcement No. 18 licensing regime on seven heavy rare earth elements (dysprosium, terbium, yttrium, scandium, samarium, gadolinium, lutetium) and derived magnets remains fully in force and was never suspended. MOFCOM's statutory review target is 45 working days (~9 weeks), but industry reporting (Duramag, gettransport.com, ~July 2026) confirms heavy-REE approvals 'routinely run past 60 to 120 days.' General licences were issued only to three named Chinese magnet makers (JL MAG, San Huan, Yunsheng) in December 2025 and are 'not broadly available.' In June 2026 MOFCOM added 10 US entities—including MP Materials and USA Rare Earth—to its export control list, denying them dual-use exports. January 2026 catalogue expansion added samarium, gadolinium and lutetium compounds. CSIS (May 2026) confirms yttrium exports to the US were only ~20 tons in February 2026 vs. 66+ tons in January 2025. Sources: gettransport.com (July 2026); CSIS analysis (May 2026); Morgan Lewis LawFlash (July 2026); Duramag market update; TechTimes (May 2026).

dissent.view: Some reporting (White House statements, Technori April 2026) frames MOFCOM's general licences to three Chinese magnet makers as a meaningful easing of approvals and a 'de facto removal' of controls for US civilian buyers, implying conditions closer to FLOWING for commercial end-users.
Rare Earth Exchanges cites licensing approval rates below 25% for European firms in some sectors (2025-2026 data), which could support a reading closer to FROZEN for those buyer segments.

items.label: Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)
items.rationale: Zero operational <=3nm capacity exists outside Taiwan as of July 2026, satisfying the rubric's EXTREME condition of '<10% outside Taiwan AND no operational overseas leading-edge fab.' The Arizona Phase 2 and Japan 3nm fabs are under construction/equipping, not in production.
items.fact: TSMC's entire <=3nm capacity (~200,000 wafers/month of N3 plus N2 ramp) is located in Taiwan (Fab 18 Tainan, Hsinchu, Kaohsiung). The only operational overseas TSMC fab - Arizona Fab 21 Phase 1 - runs N4 (4nm-class), above the rubric threshold. Arizona Phase 2 (3nm) only began equipment installation in Q3 2026, with volume production targeted H2 2027. Japan Kumamoto remains on mature nodes (N12/N6), with a 3nm upgrade not expected until 2028. Source: TSMC Q1 2026 earnings call (April 16, 2026, via TrendForce/Digitimes); CNBC TSMC CFO interview July 16-20 2026; TechTimes July 17 2026.

dissent.view: Some sources (jaredwatkins.com, April 2026) cite Arizona Fab 21 Phase 1 capacity at 90,000-100,000 wpm, far above the 10,000-30,000 wpm range cited by Industrial Info and Wikipedia - but this applies to N4, not <=3nm, so it does not change the rubric classification.

items.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: The absence of a new named encirclement drill and the measurable decline in ADIZ sortie tempo rule out Rubric 2 (crisis) and push against a pure Rubric 0 (calm); the ongoing carrier transits through the Strait, near-weekly joint combat patrols, continuing CCG assertive patrols around Taiwan's outer islands, and the July 6 SLBM test as nuclear signaling collectively maintain an elevated but sub-crisis posture consistent with Rubric 1.
items.fact: ISW/AEI China-Taiwan Update (July 17, 2026) and July 2 and July 10 updates report no new named large-scale PLA encirclement drill since 'Justice Mission 2025' (Dec 29-30, 2025). Taiwan's MND recorded 12 days in June 2026 with zero ADIZ median-line crossings and PLA sortie volumes have reverted to ~200/month from the post-Lai peak of 300+. The PLA carrier Fujian transited the Taiwan Strait (late June 2026, characterized by PRC MND as 'normal training'), and PRC-Russia 'Joint Sea-2026' exercises ran July 6-13 near Qingdao. A PLA SSBN conducted a rare SLBM test on July 6 (South Pacific impact). No kinetic clash, no blockade, and no full mobilization reported.

dissent.view: The Diplomat (Jan 3 2026) and AEI/ISW characterize the Dec 2025 Justice Mission 2025 drill as the largest since 2022 and argue PLA activity is structurally escalatory, which some analysts read as approaching crisis-threshold behavior rather than merely 'elevated'.

items.label: Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)
items.rationale: The SMW-5 fault (July 2, 2026) is a confirmed single cable disruption causing measurable connectivity degradation with rerouting underway, mapping to rubric level 1 ('single repaired/in-repair cut'). Iranian threats to Hormuz cables and ongoing shadow-fleet activity in the Baltic reinforce the THREATENED rating but no market-corroborated major severance warrants escalation to level 2.
items.fact: Pakistan's PTA reported on July 2, 2026 that the SEA-ME-WE 5 (SMW5) international submarine cable had suffered a fault causing intermittent internet degradation; traffic is being rerouted while the SMW5 consortium works to 'identify the root cause.' (Source: Dawn / PTA statement, 2 July 2026: https://www.dawn.com/news/2012491). Separately, Iran-linked media issued credible thinly-veiled warnings in April-May 2026 about vulnerability of Hormuz cables (JPOST/Jerusalem Post, 23 Apr 2026; Time, 19 May 2026), with no confirmed cut. No confirmed unrepaired major cut across Red Sea, Hormuz, or Baltic as of 20 July 2026.

dissent.view: rsinc.com characterizes a Red Sea cable cut (Seacom, TGN-Gulf, AAE-1) as a significant multi-cable event, but this appears to describe the Sept 2025 incident — now ~10 months old and likely repaired — not a current July 2026 severance.

items.label: AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)
items.rationale: Rubric Level 2 requires 'confirmed curtailment of data-center load, a declared grid emergency, or a power-scarcity-driven moratorium.' All three triggers are confirmed: DOE issued three Section 202(c) emergency orders in 2026 authorizing actual forced curtailment of data-center load in PJM; New York enacted a power-scarcity-driven statewide data-center moratorium. This is well above the Level 1 threshold of queues and delays alone.
items.fact: DOE invoked Section 202(c) of the Federal Power Act THREE times in 2026 within the PJM Interconnection, authorizing forced curtailment of data centers/large loads ≥50 MW onto backup generators: January (cold snap), May 18 (heat+maintenance outages), and June 30–July 3 (forecast peak of 166,147 MW breaking all-time record). Separately, New York Governor Hochul signed an executive order July 14, 2026 imposing a statewide moratorium on new data center permits (≥50 MW) citing 12 GW of NYISO queue load and grid strain. ERCOT queue stands at 438 GW with ~90% data-center requests (PUCT/ERCOT, June 18, 2026). IEA Electricity 2026 confirms >2,500 GW stalled in global grid queues and 'rising congestion-related curtailment.' Sources: Utility Dive (May 19, 2026); Electric Choice / DOE 202(c) orders (July 2, 2026); Axios / NY Executive Order 62 (July 14, 2026); IEA Electricity 2026 Grids chapter; PUCT/ERCOT June 18, 2026.

dissent.view: Some analysts (dev/sustainability, May 2026) argue efficiency gains and over-speculative queue filings mean actual physical curtailment impact is overstated and queue volumes are inflated by speculative requests 5-10x real demand, implying the situation may be less acute than emergency orders suggest.

items.label: China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
items.rationale: The mid-March 2026 ban on NK blends and additional phosphate varieties constitutes a NEW broadened restriction within the trailing 3 months (rubric level 2), layered on top of existing phosphate and urea controls; the tightly price-floored, volume-capped May urea quota is not a lifting of restrictions but a controlled partial release under quota. Price impact is confirmed: international urea prices up ~40% vs pre-war levels.
items.fact: In mid-March 2026, Beijing banned exports of nitrogen-potassium fertilizer blends and certain phosphate varieties (per Reuters/Bloomberg, corroborated by Exiger citing NDRC), adding to the existing NDRC-directed phosphate export suspension (in place since Dec 2025 through Aug 2026) and existing urea quotas. On April 10, 2026 China further halted sulphuric acid exports from May. A limited urea export quota of ~1.5-1.6M tonnes for June-August was issued ~May 26 with a strict $660/t price floor (Profercy, Keyuan Fertilizer, May 2026) — partial and strictly managed, not a lifting. Reuters analysis: '50-80% of Chinese fertilizer export volumes are now restricted' (customs data). Sources: Taipei Times March 21 2026, PotatoPro March 23 2026, Exiger April 16 2026, Profercy May 26 2026.

dissent.view: The Philippines reported China indicated fertilizer exports would not be restricted, though Chinese authorities never publicly confirmed this position.

items.label: Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: The rubric's Level 2 threshold ('a statewide moratorium enacted, OR >10 jurisdictions with enacted pauses/cancellations') is met on both counts simultaneously: New York has a confirmed enacted statewide executive-order moratorium (50 MW+), and well over 10 individual local jurisdictions have separately enacted construction pauses. No guesswork required — both conditions are confirmed by multiple primary news sources.
items.fact: New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed an executive order on July 14, 2026, imposing a one-year moratorium on construction of new large-scale data centers using 50 MW or more of power, making New York the first state to enact a statewide data-center moratorium (CNBC, 2026-07-14; Reuters/USNews, 2026-07-14). Separately, 100+ local pauses are enacted nationwide across >10 jurisdictions including Seattle, Spokane, Broomfield CO, Prince George's County MD, Sarasota County FL, Reno NV, Minneapolis MN, Jackson County MO, Springfield MO, McMinnville TN, Flint MI, Inver Grove Heights MN, Dodge County WI, Leeds/Homewood/Cullman/Fort Payne AL, Scarborough/Brunswick/Warren ME, and others (datacenterbans.com, 2026-07-15).

dissent.view: The NY legislative bill (S10642/A11560, 20 MW+ threshold) has NOT yet been signed into law — Governor Hochul used an executive order (50 MW+ threshold) instead, and the bill remains pending; some sources characterize the executive order as narrower in scope than the legislative measure.
Maine's legislature passed a moratorium in April 2026 but Governor Mills vetoed it, so Maine does not count as a statewide enacted moratorium.

items.label: Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)
items.rationale: A forecast surplus of +96,000 kt exceeds the +100 kt threshold of the balanced band on the surplus side, mapping cleanly to rubric band 0 (SURPLUS). The April 2026 ICSG update is the most current official forecast and supersedes the October 2025 deficit call.
items.fact: ICSG Copper Market Forecast 2026/2027 (April 2026, icsg.org press release): ICSG projects a refined copper surplus of ~96,000 metric tons in 2026, reversing its October 2025 forecast of a 150,000 mt deficit; the shift is attributed to lower-than-expected demand growth (usage now +1.6% vs prior +2.1%) and higher secondary/scrap refined production. Sources: Recycling Today (April 28 2026), ADMIS (April 24 2026), Metalnomist, IndexBox (~July 2026), icsg.org.

dissent.view: ICSG October 2025 forecast: deficit of 150,000 mt for 2026 (now superseded by April 2026 update)
J.P. Morgan: deficit of ~330,000 mt for 2026, driven by AI data-center demand (pre-April 2026 estimate, not updated post-ICSG flip)
Goldman Sachs (pre-April 2026): surplus of ~160,000 mt for 2026 — directionally aligned with latest ICSG

items.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
items.rationale: The US Southwest has confirmed D3-D4 drought on the USDM (July 16, 2026) plus a confirmed operational physical response (emergency reservoir release to protect farms, July 13, 2026), which unambiguously meets rubric level 2 ('extreme/exceptional D3-D4 OR confirmed production curtailment/water-rationing order'). Taiwan independently meets level 2 via a confirmed government water-rationing order affecting Hsinchu chip-fab hub (March 2026), with TSMC CEO warning of water constraints as recently as June 2026.
items.fact: US Drought Monitor released July 16, 2026 (droughtmonitor.unl.edu / western-water.com) shows the US Southwest 'under siege' with drought holding or worsening; as of July 14, 2026, 7.7% of the US is in D3 (Extreme) and 0.8% in D4 (Exceptional) drought. Confirmed physical intervention: 'Water managers began releasing stored water from Elkhead Reservoir on July 13, 2026, to protect Yampa River farms and endangered fish during severe drought' (western-water.com, 2026-07-18). Separately, Taiwan's Water Resources Agency imposed water rationing in March 2026 after the driest winter in 75 years, explicitly affecting chipmaking hub Hsinchu County (Taipei Times, 2026-03-27); TSMC CEO warned of water shortages in June 2026 (Reuters/Astute Group, 2026-06) though no production cutback was announced.

dissent.view: TSMC has not announced production cutbacks as of mid-2026, and monsoon forecast for late July 2026 is above-average for Southwest US, suggesting possible near-term improvement for short-term drought
Taiwan WRA rationing authorized 'until July' (expiry ambiguous); some Southwest drought improvement possible from monsoon per CPC outlook July 21-25

items.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
items.rationale: Over 5 years converts to >60 months; value is set at 60 as the floor of the stated range. The 2025 Edition (projects built through 2024) reported a median of ~55 months, confirming the upward trend now exceeding 60 months.
items.fact: LBNL Queued Up 2026 Edition (emp.lbl.gov/queues, July 2026, data through end of 2025): 'the median duration from IR to COD was over 5 years for projects built in 2025' — i.e. >60 months.

dissent.view: LBNL 2025 Edition (data through end of 2024) reported median IR-to-COD of 'over 4 years' (~55 months) for projects built in 2018-2024; Loopback Systems interpreted this as exactly 55 months average for 2024 COD projects.
Secondary source cites 'national median wait time for interconnection studies alone exceeded 40 months in 2024' — a narrower metric covering study phase only, not full IR-to-COD.

items.label: Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)
items.rationale: Fertilizer/ammonia/sulfur input flows through Hormuz are severely reduced (near-zero on AIS, ~11% of normal transits), insurance is extreme, and Iran has re-declared closure -- firmly exceeding the threshold for rating 1 (DISRUPTED). However, the rubric caps at 1 unless the strait is rated CLOSED (2) AND crude oil corroborates; Brent at $88-90 is elevated but not at crisis-peak levels, the US disputes full closure, and some dark transits continue, so the rubric ceiling of 1 applies.
items.fact: IMF PortWatch (July 12, 2026, most recent published): 10 vessel transits vs ~88/day pre-crisis (~11% of normal). WTO Trade Tracker confirms fertilizer/ammonia/sulfur outbound AIS flows 'absent or effectively at zero' post-MoU. War-risk insurance at 8x pre-crisis; Iran re-declared closure after July 8 ceasefire collapse; US reinstated naval blockade July 14 (CENTCOM). Brent crude ~$88-90/bbl (straits.live, July 19-20) -- elevated but well below the $126 March peak. Sources: IMF PortWatch; WTO DataLab Hormuz Trade Tracker; straits.live; Windward MIOC (July 19); AgBull (July 14); Wikipedia 2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis (updated July 20).

dissent.view: straits.live rates strait 'effectively closed to commercial shipping as of July 20' (Day 141), which could support a 2 if crude were also at crisis levels
iranwarlive.com (July 5) reported ~35 transits/day via temporary corridors, characterizing the strait as 'operationally open' at reduced volume -- more consistent with a contested-1 than closed-2

items.label: Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
items.rationale: At 27-29% of capacity, Lake Mead falls below the rubric's 30% CRITICAL threshold. Elevation ~1,042 ft is also below the ~1,050 ft lower intake threshold at Hoover Dam, and USBR has confirmed reduced hydropower generating capacity as a direct physical consequence - meeting the top band's requirement for a confirmed physical impact.
items.fact: USBR Lower Colorado Weekly Hydrologic Update (June 29, 2026) reports Lake Mead elevation at 1,042.77 feet and storage at 27% of capacity (usbr.gov/lc/region/g4000/weekly.pdf). Newsweek citing USBR data (~June 14-17, 2026) corroborates at 29% full with 7,487,010 acre-feet in storage.

dissent.view: lakeinsights.com reports Lake Mead at -187.06 ft vs full pool (implying ~1,042 ft elevation, consistent with USBR), noting moderate drought in watershed - no material disagreement on level, only drought severity framing.

items.label: Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: Both disputes meet Rubric Level 1: a treaty formally suspended/held in abeyance (IWT) and a treaty structurally contested without binding force (GERD/2015 Declaration), with official war-threat language and military posturing exchanged, but no confirmed kinetic action or deliberate physical flow cut-off executed as of July 2026.
items.fact: INDUS: India has held the Indus Waters Treaty in abeyance since April 2025 and India's water minister stated 'not a single drop of water will flow to Pakistan'; Pakistan's Defence Minister Khawaja Asif declared Pakistan 'would not hesitate to go to war if its water security is breached' (The Diplomat, July 7 2026; CNBC, June 22 2026). India rejected the May 15 2026 Court of Arbitration award on pondage limits, calling the court illegally constituted (Britannica, updated July 2026). GERD/NILE: No binding agreement exists; Egypt contests Ethiopia's filling of GERD as illegal; official threats (Sisi: Egypt 'will not stand idly by'; Egypt deployed military assets near Ethiopia's sea-access routes) have been exchanged but no armed clash or deliberate flow cut-off has occurred (The National, Feb 23 2026; martinplaut.com, Mar 7 2026).

dissent.view: Pakistan alleges India already carried out deliberate, abrupt flow manipulations on the Chenab (May 2026: drop from 21,887 to 5,689 cusecs), which could be interpreted as a partial operational flow cut-off approaching Level 2, but India disputes this characterisation and no independent hydrological authority has confirmed deliberate diversion.

items.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
items.rationale: The 11.57% figure is the latest published Trepp office-sector CMBS delinquency rate (June 2026 data), corroborated verbatim by MBA Newslink, YieldPro, Connect CRE, and Yahoo Finance; it maps directly to the rubric's request for the latest percent figure. This rate remains near multi-year highs (vs. Jan 2026 all-time high of 12.34%) and reflects persistent structural stress from maturing office loans with limited refinancing options.
items.fact: Trepp June 2026 CMBS report: office delinquency rate edged up 4 basis points to 11.57% — MBA Newslink, July 8 2026 (https://newslink.mba.org/mba-newslinks/2026/july/trepp-cmbs-delinquency-rate-falls-in-june/)

items.label: Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)
items.rationale: YTD insured losses of $46bn are 28% BELOW the 10-year H1 average ($64bn), failing the threshold for ELEVATED (>average); no single event exceeded $10bn, ruling out even the lower ELEVATED trigger. Both rubric conditions for a score above 0 are unmet.
items.fact: Gallagher Re H1 2026 Nat Cat & Climate Report (published ~July 15, 2026): global insured nat-cat losses totalled $46bn for H1 2026, 28% below the 10-year H1 average of $64bn and the lowest H1 total since 2018-2019; five consecutive quarters ended without a single insured loss event exceeding $10bn. Source: Gallagher Re / ajg.com/gallagherre, July 2026.

items.label: Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
items.rationale: Lecornu is governing but leads a hung-parliament minority that has repeatedly survived no-confidence votes only by thin margins and has used Article 49.3 to pass legislation — textbook Rubric 1 (FRAGILE). No dissolution, no PM resignation, and no successful confidence defeat has occurred, so Rubric 2 is not warranted.
items.fact: On 6 July 2026 the Lecornu minority government survived a no-confidence vote (132 votes in favour vs. 289 required) over its heatwave response; it previously survived motions on 14 Jan, 23 Jan, and 2 Feb 2026, and passed its 2026 budget only via Article 49.3 on 20 Jan 2026. Source: Reuters/USNews (6 Jul 2026) and Euronews/Al Jazeera (Jan-Feb 2026).

items.label: Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
items.rationale: Both TPR (primary UK regulator) and ESMA confirm buffers are adequate, leverage is reduced, and no margin-call episode or forced selling occurred in 2025-2026; this maps unambiguously to Rubric 0 (NONE). No regulator has flagged active LDI liquidity stress in 2026, and no central-bank intervention has been reported.
items.fact: TPR market oversight report (Sept 2025, data to 31 Mar 2025): 'LDI managers have maintained higher buffer thresholds, reducing leverage and improved collateral management... Interest rate volatility has returned at various moments in 2025 and the experience has been that LDI related operations have been normal.' Rate buffers raised to ~300bps (vs ~150bps pre-2022); LDI market size halved from ~GBP1.5tn to ~GBP0.7tn. ESMA TRV article (June 2026, data end-2024): 'the ability of funds to meet liquidity demands has improved compared to September 2022 after leverage-related measures under Article 25 of AIFMD were implemented.' Sources: https://www.thepensionsregulator.gov.uk/en/document-library/research-and-analysis/market-oversight-how-well-pension-schemes-are-prepared-for-ldi-risk; https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-06/ESMA50-1949966494-4208_LDI_funds_-_recent_developments_and_methods_for_liquidity_stress_testing_0.pdf

items.label: Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)
items.rationale: Two of the three major agencies (Fitch on 2025-09-12 and S&P on 2025-10-17) executed actual one-notch rating cuts from AA- to A+ within the trailing 12 months ending 2026-07-20, satisfying rubric band 2 (DOWNGRADE). Moody's negative outlook also independently satisfies band 1, but the higher band (2) takes precedence.
items.fact: Fitch downgraded France from AA- to A+ on 12 September 2025 (stable outlook); S&P downgraded France from AA-/A-1+ to A+/A-1 on 17 October 2025 (stable outlook); Moody's kept Aa3 but moved outlook to Negative on 24 October 2025. S&P affirmed A+ on 30 May 2026. Sources: S&P Global Ratings (spglobal.com, 2025-10-17), France24/Moody's (2025-10-25), Bloomberg (2026-05-30), ING Think (2025-09), internationalbanker.com (2025-09-25).

items.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
items.rationale: The trustees report worsened the depletion date by one quarter and materially widened the long-run deficit, and reform is actively debated in both chambers — squarely rubric band 1 (DEBATE). No automatic reduction has been triggered (trust fund still solvent until 2032) and no legislation enacting cuts has passed, so band 2 does not apply.
items.fact: The 2026 Social Security Trustees Report (released June 9, 2026, SSA.gov/oact/trsum/) projects the OASI Trust Fund depleted in Q4 2032 — one quarter earlier than the 2025 report — with only 78% of scheduled benefits payable at that time; the 75-year actuarial deficit widened to 4.42% of taxable payroll from 3.82% in 2025. No benefit cut has been enacted or automatically triggered. Bipartisan reform bills (PROMISE Act, Senate, July 15 2026; Bipartisan Social Security Commission Act, House, June 2026) are actively debated but none enacted.

items.label: China PPI YoY (%) - large swing either way is worse
items.rationale: The rubric scores on the SIZE of the signed move; +4.1% YoY is the steepest PPI gain since July 2022 and represents a sharp cost-push inflation reading that maps to a meaningful input-cost pressure export signal. All primary and secondary sources are in full agreement on this figure.
items.fact: China PPI for industrial products increased by 4.1% year-on-year in June 2026, per NBS official press release dated 2026-07-10 (https://www.stats.gov.cn/english/PressRelease/202607/t20260710_1964093.html).

items.label: New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)
items.rationale: At least 9 distinct new trade measures (tariffs, AD duties, broad regulatory trade barriers) were imposed or formally proposed against China by major economies in the trailing 3 months, far exceeding the rubric threshold of >=4 new measures; multiple actions constitute broad tariff rounds at sector level (tyres up to 45.3%, BDO up to 113.7%, USTR 12.5% across all China imports), unambiguously meeting the ESCALATING criterion regardless of the managed truce framework.
items.fact: In the trailing 3 months (Apr 20–Jul 20, 2026), confirmed new major-economy trade measures against China include: (1) USTR proposed 12.5% Section 301 tariff on China across broad categories (forced labor), Jun 2, 2026 (china-briefing.com/USTR); (2) EU definitive AD duties on Chinese tyres 4.3–45.3%, Jul 7, 2026 (EC policy.trade.ec.europa.eu); (3) EU definitive AD duties on BDO from China 105.6–113.7%, Jun 24, 2026 (EC policy.trade.ec.europa.eu); (4) EU definitive AD duties on glass fibre materials from China, Reg. EU 2026/1203, Jun 2026 (catts.eu); (5) EU AD investigation opened on polyether polyols from China, Jun 30, 2026 (agenceurope.eu); (6) EU registration/AD investigation on copper pipes from China effective Jun 10, 2026 (catts.eu); (7) US DOC preliminary AD/CVD circumvention findings on Chinese paper plates via Malaysia/Cambodia, Jul 10, 2026 (federalregister.gov); (8) US DOC preliminary CVD/AD on wooden cabinets from China, Jul 13, 2026 (federalregister.gov); (9) China issued Supply Chain Security and Extraterritorial Jurisdiction Regulations, Apr 7–13, 2026 (Mayer Brown July 2026). Total confirmed distinct measures: >=9, including broad tariff rounds >=10% across sectors.

items.label: US domestic RE producer blacklist (0 none / 1 entity-specific ban / 2 sector-wide ban)
items.rationale: Two named US rare-earth producers (MP Materials, USA Rare Earth) are explicitly listed on China's MOFCOM export-control entity list as of 22 June 2026, fitting rubric level 1 exactly. The restriction is firm-specific (10 named entities across sectors) and explicitly characterised by Benchmark Minerals and SMM as not a sector-wide or categorical ban, ruling out level 2.
items.fact: China MOFCOM Announcement No. 23 of 2026, issued 22 June 2026, added 10 named US entities to its export control list, including MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc. The full list: Aveox, Red Cat Holdings, Teal Drones, IMSAR, Jaia Robotics, Ball Aerospace & Technologies Corp, Oshkosh Defense, L3Harris Maritime Services, MP Materials Corp, and USA Rare Earth. Source: MOFCOM via Global Times / Benchmark Minerals / Al Jazeera / SMM (metal.com), 22 June 2026. Multiple sources and Benchmark Minerals confirm 'the measure is entity-specific rather than a blanket restriction on rare earth exports to the US.'

items.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: Index is DOWN MoM (-0.3%) and UP YoY (+2.2%), so it does not qualify as FALLING (which requires both MoM and YoY declines); MoM change is well below the 3% threshold and far below the 5% SPIKING threshold, placing it in band 1 (ELEVATED: rising or at multi-year high but MoM change <3%).
items.fact: FAO Food Price Index (FFPI) averaged 130.3 points in June 2026, down 0.3% MoM from 130.8 in May but +2.2% YoY. Source: FAO worldfoodsituation/foodpricesindex, released 3 July 2026.

items.label: Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: Three named top-20 mines have confirmed, physical production cuts active simultaneously in mid-2026—Grasberg (~59% below normal), Kamoa-Kakula (~50% below guidance), and Cobre Panama (full closure)—and major institutions (Goldman Sachs, Benchmark Mineral Intelligence) have issued deficit forecasts attributable to these outages, satisfying the CLUSTER criterion unambiguously.
items.fact: Three top-20 copper mines have confirmed, currently active production cuts: (1) Grasberg Block Cave (Indonesia, ~3% of world supply) remains on phased restart at ~65% capacity in H2 2026 after Sept 2025 mudslide; FCX Q1-2026 10-Q reports only 95M lb produced in Q1 vs. 425M lb normal, and full-year PTFI 2026 guidance cut to 0.7B lb from normal 1.7B lb (SEC filing, 2026); (2) Kamoa-Kakula (DRC, world's 3rd-largest) produced only 64,328t in Q2 2026 on 290,000–330,000t annual guidance vs. initial 600,000t target, after May 2025 seismic flooding (Ivanhoe Q2 press release, July 8 2026); (3) Cobre Panama (FQM, formerly top-10) remains closed since Nov 2023 with no restart as of July 2026, president's mid-year deadline slipped (Rio Times/riotimesonline.com, ~July 5 2026). Goldman Sachs and Benchmark Mineral Intelligence both project a 2026 copper supply deficit driven by these disruptions alone (525,000–600,000+ tonnes lost).

flags.name: Redemption gates widespread, but HY spreads benign
flags.message: Large-fund redemption gates are rated WIDESPREAD (2) but high-yield spreads are benign. Broad gating usually coincides with visible credit stress; confirm the gates are systemic, not a couple of isolated funds.

indicators.label: Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)
US high-yield OAS (%, ≈310bps)

s1_basis_trade.label: Hidden leverage in US government bonds
s1_basis_trade.gist: Hedge funds borrow heavily to trade US government bonds; if that trade unwinds fast it can freeze the market everyone treats as safe.

s2_japan.label: Japan's rates and the global cash carry trade
s2_japan.gist: Cheap Japanese money has funded bets worldwide for years; as Japan's interest rates rise, pulling that money home can ripple through global markets.

s3_private_credit.label: Private lending you can't see into
s3_private_credit.gist: A fast-growing pile of loans sits outside the banks with little public disclosure, so stress there is hard to spot until it is already large.

s4_dev_debt.label: Debt stress in developing countries
s4_dev_debt.gist: Many poorer nations owe more than they can comfortably repay; a wave of defaults can spread and pull in their lenders.

s5_china_deflation.label: China's prices spilling into the world
s5_china_deflation.gist: Big swings in China's factory prices - falling or rising - get exported to everyone else as cheaper goods or higher input costs.

s6_rare_earths.label: Rare-earth minerals used as a weapon
s6_rare_earths.gist: China controls most of the world's rare-earth processing and is now restricting it, squeezing US defense and high-tech manufacturing.

s7_stablecoins.label: Crypto dollars and US government debt
s7_stablecoins.gist: Stablecoins must hold US Treasuries as backing, so their rapid growth - or a sudden wobble - now feeds straight back into the government-debt market.

s8_ai_capex.label: The AI spending boom
s8_ai_capex.gist: Enormous borrowing to build AI data centers is racing ahead of the revenue meant to justify it, concentrating the risk in a few giant companies.

s9_cre.label: Commercial real estate and small banks
s9_cre.gist: Half-empty offices and loans coming due strain the regional banks that hold them - a slow-burning credit risk.

s10_france.label: France and eurozone stability
s10_france.gist: A politically gridlocked, heavily indebted France is the soft spot that could reopen broader euro-area stress.

s11_consumer_debt.label: Household debt strain
s11_consumer_debt.gist: Rising late payments on car loans and buy-now-pay-later show households stretching - an early warning on consumer health.

s12_gold_dedollar.label: Gold and moving away from the dollar
s12_gold_dedollar.gist: Central banks stockpiling gold and building non-dollar payment systems slowly chip away at the dollar's dominance.

s13_insurance.label: Insurers pulling back
s13_insurance.gist: As disaster losses climb, insurers raise prices or quit whole regions, leaving homes and assets exposed.

s14_pensions.label: Pensions and an aging population
s14_pensions.gist: Aging populations and underfunded pensions strain public budgets; leveraged pension funds can also break in a sudden rate shock, as the UK saw in 2022.

s15_ai_power.label: AI's strain on the power grid
s15_ai_power.gist: AI data centers need more electricity than the grid can quickly supply, triggering curtailments, building moratoria, and multi-year connection queues.

s16_copper.label: Copper for electrification
s16_copper.gist: Electrifying everything needs far more copper than mines can deliver, and major mine outages are tightening supply further.

s17_water.label: Water as a hidden constraint
s17_water.gist: Droughts and shrinking reservoirs threaten chip factories, farms, and power generation - an underpriced input with no substitute.

s18_food_fertilizer.label: Food and fertilizer supply
s18_food_fertilizer.gist: Export curbs and shipping disruptions to fertilizer raise the risk of higher food prices down the line.

s19_lithography.label: The chipmaking chokepoint
s19_lithography.gist: The most advanced chips depend on one Dutch tool-maker and Taiwan's factories; any disruption there hits the entire tech economy.

s20_sea_lanes.label: Shipping lanes and undersea cables
s20_sea_lanes.gist: Key straits like Hormuz and the undersea cables carrying the internet are vulnerable chokepoints for global trade and data.

s21_clearinghouses.label: The financial system's plumbing
s21_clearinghouses.gist: Clearinghouses sit at the center of every market; if one seized up under stress, it would jam trading everywhere at once.

playbook.disclaimer: Educational cause-and-effect only - how stress like this has historically moved through the system. Nothing here is investment advice or a prediction.

s1_basis_trade.mechanism: A rates shock forces highly leveraged government-bond arbitrage positions to unwind at once: margin calls trigger forced Treasury selling, liquidity in the world's benchmark market evaporates, and everything priced off it reprices.
benefits: cash and short-term bills through the panic
long-term government bonds AFTER central-bank intervention
s1_basis_trade.watch: The funding-spread and bond-volatility rows above; the historical pivot was the central bank announcing a backstop facility.
s1_basis_trade.trap: In past episodes the 'safe' asset itself fell first - forced sellers sell what they can - so the flight-to-safety instinct lost money until the intervention.

s2_japan.mechanism: Surging Japanese yields pull decades of cheap yen funding home: the yen spikes on repatriation, and leveraged positions worldwide that were financed in yen get liquidated together - hitting high-momentum equities and emerging markets hardest.
benefits: Japanese banks and insurers as rates normalize
s2_japan.watch: The 30-year JGB row above (live official feed) and the yen; past unwinds announced themselves through the currency.
s2_japan.trap: Historical carry unwinds were violent but brief - the 2024 episode round-tripped within days; the durable shift was in Japanese financials, not the panic.

s3_private_credit.mechanism: One honest markdown forces everyone's marks: investor withdrawals hit gates, gated funds fire-sell into an illiquid market, and lending to mid-sized companies contracts just as they need to refinance.
bleeds: private-credit vehicles trading below stated asset value
benefits: holders of dry powder buying discounted credit afterwards
public high-quality credit as the flight destination
s3_private_credit.watch: The default-rate and redemption-gate rows above; public high-yield spreads are the honest proxy for hidden marks.
s3_private_credit.trap: Opacity means the repricing arrives as a sudden gap, not a trend - by the time it is public, the move has largely happened.

s4_dev_debt.mechanism: A cluster of sovereign defaults - not one - overwhelms the restructuring machinery: redemptions from emerging-market debt funds force indiscriminate selling of strong and weak credits alike, and stress spreads through common creditors.
benefits: and historically the buyers of defaulted debt after restructuring - among the best-documented recoveries in fixed income
s4_dev_debt.watch: The new-IMF-programs row above and the dollar's strength, which is usually the upstream cause.
s4_dev_debt.trap: Index diversification means one or two defaults barely register; the systemic wave this story warns of is exactly what official bailouts exist to prevent.

s5_china_deflation.mechanism: Deepening factory-gate deflation is exported as cheaper goods, compressing margins for competing producers everywhere - unless Beijing's stimulus response whipsaws the pressure into a sudden commodity and China-equity rebound.
benefits: long-term bonds in the deflation leg
Chinese equities and industrial metals in the stimulus leg
s5_china_deflation.watch: The producer-price row above and any stimulus announcement - the two legs point opposite directions.
s5_china_deflation.trap: Policy whipsaw: historically every stimulus rumor violently reversed the deflation trade within days.

s6_rare_earths.mechanism: Export restrictions tighten into denial: magnet and heavy-rare-earth supply to defense, vehicle, and robotics manufacturers halts, panic stockpiling begins, and processing capacity outside China re-rates from commodity business to strategic asset.
bleeds: vehicle and defense production schedules in the short run
