s6_rare_earths.watch: The license-flow, blacklist, and ex-China premium rows above - the premium is the market's own severity gauge.
s6_rare_earths.trap: Historically these moves reversed as fast as they started on any truce headline, and diversified funds in the space often hold the Chinese producers too.

s7_stablecoins.mechanism: A run on a major stablecoin forces its reserves - tens of billions in short-term government debt - to liquidate within days, briefly dislocating the bill market while the crypto ecosystem's dollar rails seize and leveraged positions cascade.
benefits: historically the surviving issuer gained lasting market share after each depeg scare
s7_stablecoins.watch: The live peg-deviation and supply rows above (updated daily from the aggregator) - past runs announced themselves in the peg first.
s7_stablecoins.trap: Crypto markets have a long history of face-ripping rebounds inside downtrends; past episodes punished conviction in both directions.

s8_ai_capex.mechanism: The debt-financed buildout meets a revenue shortfall: one credit event at a leveraged data-center operator reprices all AI-adjacent borrowing, capital-spending guidance gets cut, chip orders fall, and power-demand forecasts deflate - the whole complex moves together because it was funded together.
bleeds: power producers priced on AI demand
benefits: and - the dot-com lesson - the post-bust survivors compounded for a decade
s8_ai_capex.watch: The capex-gap, revenue-gap, and refinancing rows above; credit stress historically led equity repricing.
s8_ai_capex.trap: Bubbles run longer than solvency: betting against this one early has been the era's classic ruin trade, which is why timing signals matter more than valuation.

s9_cre.mechanism: Office refinancing failures cluster into bank failures: deposit flight spreads to lookalike regional lenders, credit to small business contracts, and commercial-property marks finally reflect reality.
benefits: the largest banks via deposit inflows
and historically the whole sector rebounded within days of a backstop
s9_cre.watch: The bank-failure and office-delinquency rows above and the official problem-bank list.
s9_cre.trap: The 2023 backstop bottomed the sector in 48 hours - the policy response, not the failure, was the historical turning point.

s10_france.mechanism: Fiscal paralysis pushes French borrowing costs through crisis thresholds: banks holding the government's own bonds absorb the losses, the sovereign-bank doom loop reawakens, and stress spreads across euro-area borrowing costs.
bleeds: other high-debt euro members through contagion
benefits: and non-euro safe havens in every euro crisis
s10_france.watch: The OAT-Bund spread row above (live feed) - it is the market's single-number verdict on this story.
s10_france.trap: The central bank: euro-breakup positioning has been ruined repeatedly by ECB backstops built precisely for this scenario.

s11_consumer_debt.mechanism: Subprime stress spreads upward: rising late payments become charge-offs, lenders tighten, discretionary spending cracks, and the weakness travels from consumer lenders to retailers to the broad economy.
benefits: trade-down retailers in every past consumer squeeze
s11_consumer_debt.watch: The subprime-delinquency and BNPL rows above; the staples-versus-discretionary ratio is the market's own recession gauge.
s11_consumer_debt.trap: Lenders provision ahead of losses - their shares historically bottomed while delinquencies were still rising.

s12_gold_dedollar.mechanism: A reserve-weaponization event accelerates the slow shift: central banks swap dollar assets for gold at a faster pace, marginal foreign demand for government debt weakens, and the metal gains a structural, price-insensitive buyer.
bleeds: long-term government bonds via lost foreign demand
benefits: gold above all - the current central-bank buying era is the live example - plus non-dollar safe havens
s12_gold_dedollar.watch: The reserve-share row above (quarterly official data) and central-bank purchase reports.
s12_gold_dedollar.trap: This is measured in decades, not events - and gold miners have historically lagged the metal itself across full cycles.

s13_insurance.mechanism: A mega-catastrophe season collides with insurers already exiting exposed regions: property becomes uninsurable, collateral values fall, and the stress transmits to mortgages, regional lenders, and state backstops.
bleeds: property values and lenders in exposed regions
benefits: historically reinsurers AFTER the event as pricing hardened
and catastrophe-linked debt yields for risk-bearers
s13_insurance.watch: The insurer-exit and catastrophe-loss rows above plus the season's forecasts.
s13_insurance.trap: Positioning before the event absorbs the loss; the well-documented historical pattern favored the post-event hard market.

s14_pensions.mechanism: A rate spike forces leveraged pension strategies into collateral calls: they dump whatever is liquid, the selling pushes yields higher still, and the spiral continues until a central bank steps in - the UK lived this in 2022.
bleeds: long-term government bonds during the spiral itself
retirees' security in the slow version
benefits: whoever provided liquidity at the intervention - the 2022 central-bank entry marked the exact bottom - and retirement insurers structurally as rates rose
s14_pensions.watch: The funded-ratio row above (live monthly feed) and long-bond volatility.
s14_pensions.trap: Identical to the basis trade: buying the falling 'safe' asset before the intervention caught knives for days.

s15_ai_power.mechanism: Data-center demand outruns the grid so badly that curtailments, connection queues, and building moratoria stall the buildout - rising household bills turn it political, and the constraint moves from engineering to consent.
bleeds: utilities caught between regulators and demand
benefits: and nuclear/gas capacity - though see the trap
s15_ai_power.watch: The interconnection-wait and moratoria rows above and capacity-auction prices.
s15_ai_power.trap: The famous version of this trade assumes the buildout CONTINUES; this story's actual blow-up - moratoria stopping it - points the opposite way for the same names.

s16_copper.mechanism: A structural deficit meets a supply shock - a major mine closure removes years of planned supply - and the metal prices high enough to ration demand, raising costs for every grid, vehicle, and data-center project simultaneously.
benefits: producing miners with volume to sell
s16_copper.watch: The mine-disruption and deficit rows above and exchange inventory levels.
s16_copper.trap: The political risk that causes the shocks sits inside the producers themselves - the closed mine may be the one you were counting on.

s17_water.mechanism: Severe drought hits a chip hub, a farm belt, and cooling-dependent power at once: an unpriced input becomes a binding constraint, cutting industrial output regionally and feeding into food prices.
benefits: historically crop prices in drought years
s17_water.watch: The reservoir and drought rows above (live official feed for the largest US reservoir).
s17_water.trap: There is no pure play - broad 'water' funds are mostly global industrials, while the shock is local; the honest effects are second-order.

s18_food_fertilizer.mechanism: Fertilizer export restrictions cut application in poorer countries, next season's yields fall, grain prices spike, and food inflation becomes social and fiscal stress in import-dependent nations - looping back into the developing-world debt story.
benefits: fertilizer producers in every past squeeze
s18_food_fertilizer.watch: The FAO food-price row above (live feed) and the export-curb indicator.
s18_food_fertilizer.trap: Fertilizer stress historically reversed violently when curbs lifted, taking back most of the move.

s19_lithography.mechanism: Conflict or blockade around Taiwan stops the fabs that make nearly all leading-edge chips: every electronics supply chain seizes within a quarter, and the shock is large enough that markets themselves may not function normally.
bleeds: and anything with a chip in it
s19_lithography.watch: The tension and export-control rows above; false-alarm cycles have historically outnumbered real escalations many times over.
s19_lithography.trap: In the true scenario, settlement and market access are themselves at risk - analysis that assumes functioning markets may not apply.

s20_sea_lanes.mechanism: A chokepoint closes - this board just watched Hormuz do it - and shipping rates, war-risk insurance, and energy prices spike together while longer reroutes quietly shrink effective global capacity; cable cuts do the same for data.
s20_sea_lanes.watch: The live strait-transit feed and cable-incident rows above.
s20_sea_lanes.trap: This board's own history shows it: reopening headlines crushed the shipping move within days - persistence of the disruption was everything.

s21_clearinghouses.mechanism: A major member defaults into a volatile market and the clearinghouse's loss waterfall activates: surviving members face assessments and de-risk simultaneously, margin requirements spiral procyclically, and the plumbing everyone assumed away becomes the crisis itself.
benefits: almost nothing leveraged - which is the honest answer; cash
and physical assets have no counterparty
s21_clearinghouses.watch: The clearing-mandate rollout row above and any clearinghouse margin announcements.
s21_clearinghouses.trap: The deepest on the board: in a true clearing failure, a winning derivative position may itself not pay out.

context.intro: The famous dials, for contrast. These are the most-watched macro gauges in the world - shown here so you can compare them with this tracker's structural lens. They are not part of the score. When they disagree with the gauge above, that divergence is the story: markets price these continuously, while the risks this tracker follows are the kind markets historically ignored until they ruptured.

items.label: VIX
items.note: Expected US stock-market volatility - the most-watched fear gauge. Calm markets usually sit below ~20.

items.label: 10y-2y curve
items.note: 10-year minus 2-year Treasury yield. A negative (inverted) reading has historically preceded US recessions - with long and variable lags.

items.label: High-yield spread
items.note: The extra yield markets demand to lend to risky companies - the market's own price of credit stress. Calm is roughly below ~4.

items.label: Chicago Fed NFCI
items.note: The Chicago Fed's broad financial-conditions index across money, debt, and equity markets. Negative = looser (calmer) than average.

items.label: Unemployment
items.note: The US unemployment rate - the classic real-economy dial, updated monthly.
